# Poisson with two predictors: distance and flow

Real surveys rarely vary one thing at a time. The same Kia reef sites differ not
only in their **distance from logging** but also in their **water flow**: some
sit in strong tidal currents, others in sheltered, mild-flow water.

Flow matters ecologically. Strong currents flush logging sediment off the reef,
so the branching coral — and the juvenile bumphead parrotfish that shelter in it
— can persist even fairly close to a logging operation. In mild-flow sites the
sediment settles out and smothers the coral. So we expect flow to shift the
counts up or down *on top of* whatever the distance effect is.

## Adding a factor

`flow` is a two-level factor (`Strong`, `Mild`). FANGS auto-encodes factors to
integers in order of first appearance — here `Strong` = 1 and `Mild` = 2 — and
we turn that code into a 0/1 indicator by subtracting 1, exactly as you did with
the treatment factor earlier. That makes **strong flow the reference level**.

$$
\log(\lambda_i) = \alpha + \beta\,\text{dist}_i + \beta_{\text{flow}}\,(\text{flow}_i - 1)
$$

```
model {
  for (i in 1:N) {
    fish[i] ~ dpois(lambda[i])
    log(lambda[i]) <- alpha + beta * dist_to_logging_km[i] + beta_flow * (flow[i] - 1)
  }
  alpha     ~ dnorm(0, 10)
  beta      ~ dnorm(0, 10)
  beta_flow ~ dnorm(0, 10)
}
```

- $\alpha$ — log mean count for the **reference** site: strong flow, at zero distance from logging.
- $\beta$ — the distance effect, **holding flow constant**.
- $\beta_{\text{flow}}$ — the mild-vs-strong effect, **holding distance constant**. A negative value means mild-flow sites hold fewer fish; $e^{\beta_{\text{flow}}}$ is the multiplicative drop.

This is the additive (no-interaction) model: flow shifts the whole log-count up
or down by the same amount at every distance. If you suspected the *distance*
effect itself changed with flow, you would add an interaction term — but start
simple.

## Your task

Fit the two-predictor model to `fish-counts.csv` and enter your posterior means
for $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\beta_{\text{flow}}$. How big is the flow effect once
distance is accounted for — and which way does it go?
